header.headline: GPU Workaround Convergence
header.subtitle: Many GPU workarounds eventually converge on the same truth: memory layout and movement dominate the user-visible outcome.

left.title: Workaround stack
left.body: Sharding, offload, and fragmentation controls can buy time, but they add orchestration cost and fragility.

right.title: Convergence point
right.body: Eventually the program wants a simpler memory story, not one more orchestration layer around the same limit.

panels.title: Patch stack
panels.summary: Short-term GPU fixes often accrete as a stack of shims around the original bottleneck.
panels.note: Patches can hide the real shape.

panels.title: Memory remains first
panels.summary: When every workaround fights placement and movement, that is the message, not an implementation detail.
panels.note: The system keeps telling the same story.

panels.title: Choose the cleaner path
panels.summary: Prefer the design that simplifies the memory narrative instead of extending workaround depth forever.

callout.title: Key takeaway
points: Repeated workarounds are evidence, not progress by themselves.
Convergence usually points at the real systems constraint.
Teach the model to surface the principle, not just the patch list.
